title: 2026 Home Sellers Checklist for Victoria, BC
author: Amit Khatkar
summary: Selling a home in Victoria, BC in 2026 requires preparation, strategy, and the right data. Here's your complete checklist.
content:
Selling a home in Victoria, BC in 2026 isn't just about putting up a sign and waiting. The city operates as a unique micro-market within Vancouver Island, and buyers today are more informed than ever — many of them are using data platforms like RAG Command Center to score deals before they even contact an agent.

Before You List

  • Get a pre-listing inspection — Surprises during the buyer's inspection kill deals. Know your home's condition upfront.
  • Order a title search — Check for any encumbrances, easements, or liens that could complicate the sale.
  • Gather strata documents — If selling a condo or townhouse, you'll need Form B, meeting minutes, financial statements, and the depreciation report.
  • Complete the Property Disclosure Statement — This is legally required in BC and protects both you and the buyer.

Pricing Strategy

Victoria's market varies dramatically by neighbourhood. A home in Oak Bay commands a very different $/sqft than one in Langford. Our deal scoring engine benchmarks every listing against area medians — overpriced homes get flagged as stale, while well-priced ones surface as top deals. Price strategically from day one.

Marketing Your Home

  • Professional photography and virtual tours are non-negotiable
  • Social media advertising (Facebook/Instagram) targeted to active buyers in Victoria
  • Listing syndication across RAG platform, MLS, and partner networks
  • Open houses and targeted showings for pre-qualified buyers

The Victoria market rewards preparation and data-driven pricing. Work with an agent who understands both.

title: How RAG Deal Scores Work — And Why They Matter
author: Gary Doman
summary: Every listing on RAG gets a transparent 0-100% deal score. Here's exactly how it's calculated.
content:
Every listing on the RAG Command Center receives a composite deal score between 0 and 100%. Unlike black-box algorithms at other platforms, our scoring is fully transparent — you can see exactly which components contribute to each score and why.

The Six Components

  • Below Market (35% weight) — We calculate $/sqft and compare it to the area median for the same city and property type. Listings priced significantly below the median score higher.
  • Price Drop (20% weight) — Recent and large price reductions indicate motivated sellers. Both the magnitude and recency of drops factor into the score.
  • Days on Market (15% weight) — Fresh listings score higher for urgency, while high-DOM listings score higher for negotiation leverage. The engine weighs both signals.
  • Area Comps (15% weight) — The density of comparable listings in the area. More comps means more confidence in the market position assessment.
  • Features (10% weight) — A bed/bath utility score based on the property's livability features relative to its price point.
  • Data Freshness (5% weight) — How recent the source data is. Stale data gets penalized.

Source Reconciliation

Before scoring, every listing goes through our multi-source reconciliation pipeline. When different sources disagree on a field (e.g., one says 3 beds, another says 4), we flag the conflict and use authority tiers to pick the canonical value. The deal score is always calculated on reconciled, verified data.

This is the advantage of data intelligence over raw MLS feeds — you get a cleaner, more trustworthy signal.

title: BC First-Time Home Buyer Programs in 2026
author: Ricki Kohli
summary: A complete guide to the programs, exemptions, and incentives available to first-time buyers in British Columbia.
content:
If you're buying your first home in British Columbia, there are several government programs designed to help reduce your upfront costs. Here's what's available in 2026.

Property Transfer Tax (PTT) Exemption

First-time buyers in BC may be exempt from the Property Transfer Tax on homes up to $500,000 (with a partial exemption up to $525,000). The property must be your primary residence, and you must be a Canadian citizen or permanent resident.

First Home Savings Account (FHSA)

The federal FHSA lets you save up to $8,000 per year (lifetime max $40,000) in a tax-advantaged account specifically for your first home purchase. Contributions are tax-deductible, and withdrawals for a qualifying home are tax-free.

Home Buyers' Plan (HBP)

You can withdraw up to $35,000 from your RRSP to put toward your first home (tax-free, as long as you repay over 15 years). This can be combined with the FHSA.

GST/HST New Housing Rebate

If buying a newly built home, you may qualify for a partial rebate of the GST paid on the purchase price (for homes under $450,000).

CMHC Insurance

With as little as 5% down, you can purchase a home with CMHC mortgage insurance. While the premium adds to your mortgage, it enables home ownership much sooner. Use our Mortgage Calculator to see the impact on your payments.
